infobox.value: |  |   | --- | --- |   | [Afrikaans](/wiki/Afrikaans "Afrikaans"): | *Republiek van Namibië*[[1]](#cite_note-1) |   | [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): | *Republik Namibia*[[2]](#cite_note-2) |   | [Khoekhoegowab](/wiki/Khoekhoe_language "Khoekhoe language"): | *Republiki Namibiab dib*[[3]](#cite_note-3) |   | [Oshiwambo](/wiki/Owambo_language "Owambo language"): | *Orepublika yaNamibia*[[4]](#cite_note-4) |   | [Otjiherero](/wiki/Herero_language "Herero language"): | *Orepublika yaNamibia*[[5]](#cite_note-5) |   | [RuKwangali](/wiki/Kwangali_language "Kwangali language"): | *Republika zaNamibia*[[6]](#cite_note-6) |   | [Setswana](/wiki/Tswana_language "Tswana language"): | *Rephaboliki ya Namibia*[[7]](#cite_note-7) |   | [siLozi](/wiki/Lozi_language "Lozi language"): | *Namibia ye Lukuluhile*[[8]](#cite_note-8) | |

* [Afrikaans](/wiki/Afrikaans "Afrikaans") * [German](/wiki/German_language_in_Namibia "German language in Namibia") * [Khoekhoegowab](/wiki/Khoekhoe_language "Khoekhoe language") * [Oshiwambo](/wiki/Owambo_language "Owambo language") * [Otjiherero](/wiki/Herero_language "Herero language") * [RuKwangali](/wiki/Kwangali_language "Kwangali language") * [Setswana](/wiki/Tswana_language "Tswana language") * [siLozi](/wiki/Lozi_language "Lozi language")

* 93.2% [Indigenous African](/wiki/Demographics_of_Namibia "Demographics of Namibia")[[A]](#cite_note-11) * 3.6% [Coloured](/wiki/Coloured_people_in_Namibia "Coloured people in Namibia") and [Basters](/wiki/Basters "Basters") * 1.8% [White](/wiki/White_Namibians "White Namibians") * 1.4% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_people "Asian people") / other

* + 87.9% [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity_in_Namibia "Christianity in Namibia")     - 43.7% [Lutheranism](/wiki/Evangelical_Lutheran_Church_in_Namibia "Evangelical Lutheran Church in Namibia")     - 22.8% [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Namibia "Catholic Church in Namibia")     - 17.0% [Anglicanism](/wiki/Diocese_of_Namibia "Diocese of Namibia")     - 4.4% other [Christian](/wiki/List_of_Christian_denominations "List of Christian denominations") * 10.5% other * 1.6% [no religion](/wiki/Irreligion "Irreligion")

Unitary [semi-presidential republic](/wiki/Semi-presidential_system "Semi-presidential system")[[12]](#cite_note-Dual-14)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]
825,615 km2 (318,772 sq mi) ([34th](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_area "List of countries and dependencies by area"))
Neutral increase 3,022,401[[13]](#cite_note-15)[[10]](#cite_note-Census2023-10) ([136th](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_population "List of countries and dependencies by population"))
Increase $12,370[[14]](#cite_note-auto1-16) ([117th](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP)_per_capita "List of countries by GDP (PPP) per capita"))
Increase 0.665[[16]](#cite_note-HDI-18) medium ([136th](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_Human_Development_Index "List of countries by Human Development Index"))
[Namibian dollar](/wiki/Namibian_dollar "Namibian dollar")   (NAD)   [South African rand](/wiki/South_African_rand "South African rand") (ZAR)

**Namibia**,[[C]](#cite_note-22) officially the **Republic of Namibia**, is a country in [Southern Africa](/wiki/Southern_Africa "Southern Africa").[[20]](#cite_note-23) It borders the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") to the west, [Angola](/wiki/Angola "Angola") and [Zambia](/wiki/Zambia "Zambia") to the north, [Botswana](/wiki/Botswana "Botswana") to the east and [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") to the south; in the northeast, approximating a [quadripoint](/wiki/Quadripoint "Quadripoint"),[[21]](#cite_note-24) [Zimbabwe](/wiki/Zimbabwe "Zimbabwe") lies less than 200 metres (660 feet) away along the [Zambezi](/wiki/Zambezi "Zambezi") river near [Kazungula](/wiki/Kazungula "Kazungula"), Zambia. Namibia's capital and largest city is [Windhoek](/wiki/Windhoek "Windhoek").

Namibia is the driest country in [sub-Saharan Africa](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa"),[[22]](#cite_note-25) and has been inhabited since prehistoric times by the [Khoi](/wiki/Khoekhoe "Khoekhoe"), [San](/wiki/San_people "San people"), [Damara](/wiki/Damara_people "Damara people") and [Nama people](/wiki/Nama_people "Nama people"). Around the 14th century, [immigrating](/wiki/Immigration "Immigration") [Bantu peoples](/wiki/Bantu_peoples "Bantu peoples") arrived as part of the [Bantu expansion](/wiki/Bantu_expansion "Bantu expansion"). From 1600 the [Owambo](/wiki/Owambo_people#History "Owambo people") formed kingdoms, such as [Ondonga](/wiki/Ondonga "Ondonga") and [Oukwanyama](/wiki/Oukwanyama "Oukwanyama").[[23]](#cite_note-Frieda_1991-26) In 1884, the [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") established rule over most of the territory, forming a colony known as [German South West Africa](/wiki/German_South_West_Africa "German South West Africa"). Between 1904-08, German troops waged a punitive campaign against the [Herero](/wiki/Herero_people "Herero people") and [Nama](/wiki/Nama_people "Nama people") which escalated into the [first genocide](/wiki/Herero_and_Nama_genocide "Herero and Nama genocide") of the 20th century. German rule ended during the First World War with a 1915 defeat by [South African](/wiki/Union_of_South_Africa "Union of South Africa") forces. In 2021, German and Namibian diplomats created a "reconciliation agreement" acknowledging atrocities from the German colonial period.[[24]](#cite_note-27)

In 1920 the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") [mandated](/wiki/League_of_Nations_mandate "League of Nations mandate") administration of the colony to South Africa. The [National Party](/wiki/National_Party_(South_Africa) "National Party (South Africa)"), elected to power in 1948 in South Africa, applied [apartheid](/wiki/Apartheid "Apartheid") to what was then known as [South West Africa](/wiki/South_West_Africa "South West Africa"). Uprisings and demands for political representation resulted in the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") assuming direct responsibility over the territory in 1966, but South Africa maintained *de facto* rule until 1973. That year the UN recognised the *South West Africa People's Organisation*, [SWAPO](/wiki/SWAPO "SWAPO"), as the official representative of the Namibian people. Namibia gained independence from South Africa in March 1990, following the [South African Border War](/wiki/South_African_Border_War "South African Border War"). However, [Walvis Bay](/wiki/Walvis_Bay "Walvis Bay") and the [Penguin Islands](/wiki/Penguin_Islands "Penguin Islands") remained under South African control until 1994.

Namibia is a [parliamentary democracy](/wiki/Parliamentary_democracy "Parliamentary democracy"). Agriculture, [tourism](/wiki/Tourism_in_Namibia "Tourism in Namibia") and the [mining industry](/wiki/Mining_in_Namibia "Mining in Namibia") – including mining for diamonds, [uranium](/wiki/Uranium_mining "Uranium mining"), [gold](/wiki/Gold_mining "Gold mining"), [silver](/wiki/Silver_mining "Silver mining") and [base metals](/wiki/Base_metal "Base metal") – form the basis of its [economy](/wiki/Economy_of_Namibia "Economy of Namibia"), the manufacturing sector is comparatively small. Despite significant [GDP](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") growth since its independence,[[25]](#cite_note-28) [poverty](/wiki/Poverty_in_Namibia "Poverty in Namibia") and [inequality](/wiki/Wealth_inequality "Wealth inequality") remain significant. 41% of the population is affected by [multidimensional poverty](/wiki/Multidimensional_Poverty_Index "Multidimensional Poverty Index"),[[26]](#cite_note-:5-29) and more than 400,000 people live in [informal housing](/wiki/Informal_housing "Informal housing").[[27]](#cite_note-30) [Income disparity](/wiki/Income_disparity "Income disparity") is one of the world's highest with a [Gini coefficient](/wiki/Gini_coefficient "Gini coefficient") of 59 in 2015.[[28]](#cite_note-:6-31)

With a population of 3.1 million people, Namibia is one of the [most sparsely populated](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_by_population_density "List of countries and territories by population density") countries in the world. Since the end of the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"), it has attracted notable immigration from Germany, Angola, and Zimbabwe.[[29]](#cite_note-32) Namibia is a member state of the [United Nations,](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") the [Southern African Development Community](/wiki/Southern_African_Development_Community "Southern African Development Community"), the [African Union](/wiki/African_Union "African Union") and the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Namibia](/wiki/History_of_Namibia "History of Namibia")

### Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The name of the country is derived from the [Namib](/wiki/Namib "Namib") desert, the oldest desert in the world.[[30]](#cite_note-at1315-33) The word *Namib* itself is of [Khoi](/wiki/Khoikhoi_language "Khoikhoi language") origin and means "vast place". The name was chosen by [Mburumba Kerina](/wiki/Mburumba_Kerina "Mburumba Kerina"), who originally proposed "Republic of Namib".[[31]](#cite_note-34) Before Namibia became independent in 1990, its territory was known first as [German South-West Africa](/wiki/German_South-West_Africa "German South-West Africa") (*Deutsch-Südwestafrika*), and then as [South West Africa](/wiki/South_West_Africa "South West Africa"), reflecting its colonial occupation by Germans and South Africans, respectively.

### Pre-colonial period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Pre-colonial period")]

*Citation from the book Namibia by Pascal Belda:*

"The dry lands of Namibia have been inhabited since prehistoric times by the [San](/wiki/San_people "San people"), [Damara](/wiki/Damara_people "Damara people"), and [Nama](/wiki/Nama_people "Nama people"). For thousands of years, the [Khoisan](/wiki/Khoisan "Khoisan") peoples of Southern Africa maintained a [nomadic](/wiki/Nomad "Nomad") life, the [Khoikhoi](/wiki/Khoikhoi "Khoikhoi") as pastoralists and the San people as [hunter-gatherers](/wiki/Hunter-gatherer "Hunter-gatherer"). Around the 14th century, immigrating [Bantu people](/wiki/Bantu_people "Bantu people") began to arrive during the [Bantu expansion](/wiki/Bantu_expansion "Bantu expansion") from central Africa." [[32]](#cite_note-35)

From the late 18th century onward, [Oorlam people](/wiki/Oorlam_people "Oorlam people") from Cape Colony crossed the [Orange River](/wiki/Orange_River "Orange River") and moved into the area that today is southern Namibia.[[33]](#cite_note-KDA-36) Their encounters with the nomadic Nama tribes were largely peaceful. They received the missionaries accompanying the Oorlam very well,[[34]](#cite_note-Dierks-37) granting them the right to use waterholes and grazing against an annual payment.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVedder1997177-38) On their way further north, however, the Oorlam encountered clans of the [OvaHerero](/wiki/OvaHerero_people "OvaHerero people") at Windhoek, [Gobabis](/wiki/Gobabis "Gobabis"), and [Okahandja](/wiki/Okahandja "Okahandja"), who resisted their encroachment. The Nama-Herero War broke out in 1880, with hostilities ebbing only after the [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") deployed troops to the contested places and cemented the status quo among the Nama, Oorlam, and Herero.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVedder1997659-39)

In 1878, the [Cape of Good Hope](/wiki/Cape_Colony "Cape Colony"), then a British colony, annexed the port of Walvis Bay and the offshore [Penguin Islands](/wiki/Penguin_Islands "Penguin Islands"); these became an integral part of the new [Union of South Africa](/wiki/Union_of_South_Africa "Union of South Africa") at its creation in 1910.

The first Europeans to disembark and explore the region were the Portuguese navigators [Diogo Cão](/wiki/Diogo_C%C3%A3o "Diogo Cão") in 1485[[37]](#cite_note-40) and [Bartolomeu Dias](/wiki/Bartolomeu_Dias "Bartolomeu Dias") in 1486, but the Portuguese did not try to claim the area. Like most of the interior of [Sub-Saharan Africa](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa"), Namibia was not extensively explored by Europeans until the 19th century. At that time traders and settlers came principally from Germany and Sweden. In 1870, [Finnish](/wiki/Finland "Finland") missionaries came to the northern part of Namibia to spread the [Lutheran religion](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") among the [Owambo](/wiki/Owambo_people "Owambo people") and [Kavango people](/wiki/Kavango_people "Kavango people").[[38]](#cite_note-finnish-mission-41) In the late 19th century, [Dorsland Trekkers](/wiki/Dorsland_Trek "Dorsland Trek") crossed the area on their way from the [Transvaal](/wiki/South_African_Republic "South African Republic") to Angola. Some of them settled in Namibia instead of continuing their journey.

### German rule

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: German rule")]

See also: [German South West Africa](/wiki/German_South_West_Africa "German South West Africa") and [Herero and Nama genocide](/wiki/Herero_and_Nama_genocide "Herero and Nama genocide")

Namibia became a German colony in 1884 under [Otto von Bismarck](/wiki/Otto_von_Bismarck "Otto von Bismarck") to forestall perceived British encroachment and was known as [German South West Africa](/wiki/German_South_West_Africa "German South West Africa") (*Deutsch-Südwestafrika*).[[39]](#cite_note-42) The [Palgrave Commission](/wiki/Palgrave_Commission "Palgrave Commission") by the British governor in [Cape Town](/wiki/Cape_Town "Cape Town") determined that only the natural deep-water harbour of Walvis Bay was worth occupying and thus annexed it to the Cape province of British South Africa.

In 1897, a [rinderpest epidemic](/wiki/1890s_African_rinderpest_epizootic "1890s African rinderpest epizootic") caused massive cattle die-offs of an estimated 95% of cattle in southern and central Namibia. In response the German colonisers set up a veterinary [cordon](/wiki/Cordon_sanitaire_(medicine) "Cordon sanitaire (medicine)") fence known as the [Red Line](/wiki/Red_Line_(Namibia) "Red Line (Namibia)").[[40]](#cite_note-:7-43) In 1907 this fence then broadly defined the boundaries for the first Police Zone.[[41]](#cite_note-44)

From 1904 to 1907, the [Herero](/wiki/Herero_people "Herero people") and the [Nama](/wiki/Nama_people "Nama people") [took up arms](/wiki/Herero_Wars "Herero Wars") against ruthless German settlers. In a calculated punitive action by the German settlers, [government](/wiki/Government_of_Namibia "Government of Namibia") officials ordered the extinction of the natives in the OvaHerero and Nama genocide. In what has been called the "first genocide of the 20th century",[[42]](#cite_note-45) the Germans systematically killed 10,000 Nama (half the population) and approximately 65,000 Herero (about 80% of the population).[[43]](#cite_note-46)[[44]](#cite_note-Adhikari-47) Released from detention, the survivors faced a system of forced labor, deportation, and [apartheid](/wiki/Apartheid "Apartheid")-like segregation that foreshadowed South Africa's 1948 policy. Most Africans were confined to so-called native territories, which under South African rule after 1949 were turned into "homelands" ([Bantustans](/wiki/Bantustans "Bantustans")).

Some historians have speculated that the downfall of the Herero in Namibia was a model for the [Nazis](/wiki/Nazism "Nazism") in [the Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust").[[45]](#cite_note-Madley-48) The memory of what happened under German rule has contributed to shape the ethnic identity in independent Namibia and has kept its significance in today's relations with Germany.[[46]](#cite_note-49)

The German minister for development aid apologised for the Namibian genocide in 2004. However, the German government distanced itself from this apology.[[47]](#cite_note-50) Only in 2021 did the [German government](/wiki/Government_of_Germany "Government of Germany") acknowledge the genocide and agree to pay €1.1 billion over 30 years in community aid.[[48]](#cite_note-51)

### South African mandate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: South African mandate")]

See also: [South West Africa](/wiki/South_West_Africa "South West Africa")

During [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), South African troops under General [Louis Botha](/wiki/Louis_Botha "Louis Botha") [occupied the territory](/wiki/South_West_Africa_campaign "South West Africa campaign") and deposed the German colonial administration. The end of the war and the [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles") resulted in South West Africa remaining a possession of South Africa, at first as a [League of Nations mandate](/wiki/League_of_Nations_mandate "League of Nations mandate"), until 1990.[[49]](#cite_note-Rajagopal-52) The mandate system was formed as a compromise between those who advocated for an allied annexation of former German and Ottoman territories and a proposition put forward by those who wished to grant them to an international trusteeship until they could govern themselves.[[49]](#cite_note-Rajagopal-52) It permitted the South African government to administer South West Africa until that territory's inhabitants were prepared for political self-determination.[[50]](#cite_note-Louis-53) South Africa interpreted the mandate as a veiled annexation and made no attempt to prepare South West Africa for future autonomy.[[50]](#cite_note-Louis-53)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Witbooi_Hendrik.jpg/250px-Witbooi_Hendrik.jpg)](/wiki/File:Witbooi_Hendrik.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/SamuelMaharero.jpg/250px-SamuelMaharero.jpg)](/wiki/File:SamuelMaharero.jpg)

[Hendrik Witbooi](/wiki/Hendrik_Witbooi_(Nama_chief) "Hendrik Witbooi (Nama chief)") (left) and [Samuel Maharero](/wiki/Samuel_Maharero "Samuel Maharero") (right) were prominent leaders against German colonial rule.

As a result of the [Conference on International Organization](/wiki/United_Nations_Conference_on_International_Organization "United Nations Conference on International Organization") in 1945, the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") was formally superseded by the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") (UN) and former League mandates by a trusteeship system. Article 77 of the [United Nations Charter](/wiki/United_Nations_Charter "United Nations Charter") stated that UN trusteeship "shall apply...to territories now held under mandate"; furthermore, it would "be a matter of subsequent agreement as to which territories in the foregoing territories will be brought under the trusteeship system and under what terms".[[51]](#cite_note-Vandenbosch-54) The UN requested all former League of Nations mandates be surrendered to its [Trusteeship Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Trusteeship_Council "United Nations Trusteeship Council") in anticipation of their independence.[[51]](#cite_note-Vandenbosch-54) South Africa declined to do so and instead requested permission from the UN to formally annex South West Africa, for which it received considerable criticism.[[51]](#cite_note-Vandenbosch-54) When the UN General Assembly rejected this proposal, South Africa dismissed its opinion and began solidifying control of the territory.[[51]](#cite_note-Vandenbosch-54) The UN General Assembly and Security Council responded by referring the issue to the [International Court of Justice](/wiki/International_Court_of_Justice "International Court of Justice") (ICJ), which held a number of discussions on the legality of South African rule between 1949 and 1966.[[52]](#cite_note-First-55)

South Africa began imposing *[apartheid](/wiki/Apartheid "Apartheid"),* its [codified](/wiki/Codification_(law) "Codification (law)") system of racial segregation and discrimination, on South West Africa during the late 1940s.[[53]](#cite_note-Crawford1-56) Black South West Africans were subject to [pass laws](/wiki/Pass_laws "Pass laws"), curfews, and a host of residential regulations that restricted their movement.[[53]](#cite_note-Crawford1-56) Development was concentrated in the southern region of the territory adjacent to South Africa, known as the "[Police Zone](/w/index.php?title=Police_Zone_(South_West_Africa)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Police Zone (South West Africa) (page does not exist)")", where most of the major settlements and commercial economic activity were located.[[54]](#cite_note-Devils-57) Outside the Police Zone, indigenous peoples were restricted to theoretically self-governing [tribal homelands](/wiki/Bantustan#Bantustans_in_South_West_Africa "Bantustan").[[54]](#cite_note-Devils-57)

During the late 1950s and early 1960s, the accelerated [decolonisation of Africa](/wiki/Decolonisation_of_Africa "Decolonisation of Africa") and mounting pressure on the remaining colonial powers to grant their colonies self-determination resulted in the formation of nascent nationalist parties in South West Africa.[[55]](#cite_note-Müller-58) Movements such as the [South West African National Union](/wiki/South_West_African_National_Union "South West African National Union") (SWANU) and the [South West African People's Organisation](/wiki/South_West_African_People%27s_Organisation "South West African People's Organisation") (SWAPO) advocated for the formal termination of South Africa's mandate and independence for the territory.[[55]](#cite_note-Müller-58) In 1966, following the ICJ's controversial ruling that it had no legal standing to consider the question of South African rule, SWAPO launched an armed insurgency that escalated into part of a wider regional conflict known as the [South African Border War](/wiki/South_African_Border_War "South African Border War").[[56]](#cite_note-Caprivi-59)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Foreign_Observer_identification_badge_in_the_1989_Namibian_election.jpg/250px-Foreign_Observer_identification_badge_in_the_1989_Namibian_election.jpg)](/wiki/File:Foreign_Observer_identification_badge_in_the_1989_Namibian_election.jpg)

Foreign Observer identification badge issued during the 1989 Namibian election

In 1971 Namibian contract workers led a [general strike against the contract system](/wiki/1971%E2%80%9372_Namibian_contract_workers_strike "1971–72 Namibian contract workers strike") and in support of independence.[[57]](#cite_note-Moorsom1979-60) Some of the striking workers would later join SWAPO's [PLAN](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army_of_Namibia "People's Liberation Army of Namibia")[[58]](#cite_note-Devils2-61) as part of the South African Border War.

### Independence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Independence")]

As SWAPO's insurgency intensified, South Africa's case for annexation in the international community continued to decline.[[59]](#cite_note-Dobell-62) The UN declared that South Africa had failed in its obligations to ensure the moral and material well-being of South West Africa's indigenous inhabitants, and had thus disavowed its own mandate.[[60]](#cite_note-Yusuf-63) On 12 June 1968, the [UN General Assembly](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly "United Nations General Assembly") adopted a resolution proclaiming that, in accordance with the desires of its people, South West Africa be renamed *Namibia*.[[60]](#cite_note-Yusuf-63) [United Nations Security Council Resolution 269](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council_Resolution_269 "United Nations Security Council Resolution 269"), adopted in August 1969, declared South Africa's continued occupation of Namibia illegal.[[60]](#cite_note-Yusuf-63)[[61]](#cite_note-MAA-64) In recognition of this landmark decision, SWAPO's armed wing was renamed the [People's Liberation Army of Namibia](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army_of_Namibia "People's Liberation Army of Namibia") (PLAN).[[62]](#cite_note-Camp-65)

Namibia became one of several flashpoints for [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") proxy conflicts in southern Africa during the years of the PLAN insurgency.[[63]](#cite_note-Hughes-66) The insurgents sought out weapons and sent recruits to the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") for military training.[[64]](#cite_note-Betram-67) As the PLAN war effort gained momentum, the Soviet Union and other states such as Cuba continued to increase their support, deploying advisers to train the insurgents directly as well as supplying more weapons and ammunition.[[65]](#cite_note-Vanneman-68) SWAPO's leadership, dependent on Soviet, Angolan, and Cuban military aid, positioned the movement firmly within the socialist bloc by 1975.[[66]](#cite_note-Dreyer-69) This practical alliance reinforced the external perception of SWAPO as a Soviet proxy, which dominated Cold War rhetoric in South Africa and the United States.[[54]](#cite_note-Devils-57) For its part, the Soviet Union supported SWAPO partly because it viewed South Africa as a regional Western ally.[[67]](#cite_note-Shultz-70)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/SADF-Operations_4.jpg/250px-SADF-Operations_4.jpg)](/wiki/File:SADF-Operations_4.jpg)

South African troops patrol the border region for PLAN insurgents, 1980s.

Growing war weariness and the reduction of tensions between the superpowers compelled South Africa, Angola, and Cuba to accede to the [Tripartite Accord](/wiki/Tripartite_Accord_(Angola) "Tripartite Accord (Angola)"), under pressure from both the Soviet Union and the United States.[[68]](#cite_note-SACP-71) South Africa accepted Namibian independence in exchange for Cuban military withdrawal from the region and an Angolan commitment to cease all aid to PLAN.[[69]](#cite_note-James-72) PLAN and South Africa adopted an informal ceasefire in August 1988, and a [United Nations Transition Assistance Group](/wiki/United_Nations_Transition_Assistance_Group "United Nations Transition Assistance Group") (UNTAG) was formed to monitor the Namibian peace process and supervise the return of refugees.[[70]](#cite_note-Sitkowski-73) The ceasefire ended after PLAN made a final incursion into the territory, possibly as a result of misunderstanding UNTAG's directives, in March 1989.[[71]](#cite_note-Clairborne-74) A new ceasefire was later imposed with the condition that the insurgents were to be confined to their external bases in Angola until they could be disarmed and demobilised by UNTAG.[[70]](#cite_note-Sitkowski-73)[[72]](#cite_note-Demob-75)

By the end of the 11-month transition period, the last South African troops had been withdrawn from Namibia, all political prisoners granted amnesty, racially discriminatory legislation repealed, and 42,000 Namibian refugees returned to their homes.[[66]](#cite_note-Dreyer-69) Just over 97% of eligible voters participated in the country's first [parliamentary elections](/wiki/Namibian_parliamentary_election,_1989 "Namibian parliamentary election, 1989") held under a [universal franchise](/wiki/Universal_franchise "Universal franchise").[[73]](#cite_note-NYT1989-76) The United Nations plan included oversight by [foreign election observers](/wiki/Election_monitoring "Election monitoring") in an effort to ensure a [free and fair election](/wiki/Election#Non-democratic_elections "Election"). SWAPO won a plurality of seats in the [Constituent Assembly](/wiki/Members_of_the_Constituent_Assembly_of_Namibia "Members of the Constituent Assembly of Namibia") with 57% of the popular vote.[[73]](#cite_note-NYT1989-76) This gave the party 41 seats, but not a two-thirds majority, which would have enabled it to draft the constitution on its own.[[73]](#cite_note-NYT1989-76)

The [Namibian Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Namibia "Constitution of Namibia") was adopted in February 1990. It incorporated protection for human rights and compensation for state expropriations of private property and established an independent judiciary, legislature, and an executive presidency (the constituent assembly became the national assembly). The country officially became independent on 21 March 1990.[[74]](#cite_note-77)[[38]](#cite_note-finnish-mission-41) [Sam Nujoma](/wiki/Sam_Nujoma "Sam Nujoma") was sworn in as the first [President of Namibia](/wiki/President_of_Namibia "President of Namibia") at a ceremony attended by [Nelson Mandela](/wiki/Nelson_Mandela "Nelson Mandela") of South Africa (who had been released from prison the previous month) and representatives from 147 countries, including 20 heads of state.[[75]](#cite_note-78) In 1994, shortly before the first multiracial elections in South Africa, that country ceded Walvis Bay to Namibia.[[76]](#cite_note-79)

### After independence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: After independence")]

Since independence Namibia has completed the transition from white minority apartheid rule to parliamentary democracy. [Multiparty democracy](/wiki/Multiparty_democracy "Multiparty democracy") was introduced and has been maintained, with local, regional and [national elections](/wiki/Elections_in_Namibia "Elections in Namibia") held regularly. Several registered political parties are active and represented in the National Assembly, although the [SWAPO](/wiki/SWAPO "SWAPO") has won every election since independence.[[77]](#cite_note-80) The transition from the 15-year rule of President [Nujoma](/wiki/Sam_Nujoma "Sam Nujoma") to his successor [Hifikepunye Pohamba](/wiki/Hifikepunye_Pohamba "Hifikepunye Pohamba") in 2005 went smoothly.[[78]](#cite_note-IRIN-81)

Since independence, the Namibian government has promoted a policy of national reconciliation. It issued an amnesty for those who fought on either side during the liberation war. The civil war in Angola spilled over and adversely affected Namibians living in the north of the country. In 1998, [Namibia Defence Force](/wiki/Namibia_Defence_Force "Namibia Defence Force") (NDF) troops were sent to the [Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Democratic Republic of the Congo") as part of a [Southern African Development Community](/wiki/Southern_African_Development_Community "Southern African Development Community") (SADC) contingent. In 1999, the national government quashed a secessionist attempt in the northeastern [Caprivi Strip](/wiki/Caprivi_Strip "Caprivi Strip").[[78]](#cite_note-IRIN-81) The [Caprivi conflict](/wiki/Caprivi_conflict "Caprivi conflict") was initiated by the [Caprivi Liberation Army](/wiki/Caprivi_Liberation_Army "Caprivi Liberation Army") (CLA), a [rebel](/wiki/Rebellion "Rebellion") group led by [Mishake Muyongo](/wiki/Mishake_Muyongo "Mishake Muyongo"). It wanted the Caprivi Strip to secede and form its own society.[[79]](#cite_note-82)

In 2007, [Twyfelfontein](/wiki/Twyfelfontein "Twyfelfontein") was inscribed as a cultural [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site"), a prehistoric site with one of the largest concentrations of rock engravings on the African continent.[[80]](#cite_note-83) In December 2014, Prime Minister [Hage Geingob](/wiki/Hage_Geingob "Hage Geingob"), the candidate of ruling SWAPO, won the [presidential elections](/wiki/2014_Namibian_general_election "2014 Namibian general election"), taking 87% of the vote. His predecessor, President [Hifikepunye Pohamba](/wiki/Hifikepunye_Pohamba "Hifikepunye Pohamba"), also of SWAPO, had served the maximum two terms allowed by the constitution.[[81]](#cite_note-84) In December 2019, President Hage Geingob was [re-elected](/wiki/2019_Namibian_general_election "2019 Namibian general election") for a second term, taking 56.3% of the vote.[[82]](#cite_note-85) On 4 February 2024, President Hage Geingob died and he was immediately succeeded by vice-president [Nangolo Mbumba](/wiki/Nangolo_Mbumba "Nangolo Mbumba") as new President of Namibia who finished the late President's term as it came to an end in March 2025.[[83]](#cite_note-86) SWAPO's first female presidential candidate, [Netumbo Nandi-Ndaitwah](/wiki/Netumbo_Nandi-Ndaitwah "Netumbo Nandi-Ndaitwah"), was declared the winner of the [2024 elections](/wiki/2024_Namibian_general_election "2024 Namibian general election") with 57% of the vote.[[84]](#cite_note-87) On 21 March 2025, she was sworn in as Namibia's new president.[[85]](#cite_note-88)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Geography")]

Main article: [Geography of Namibia](/wiki/Geography_of_Namibia "Geography of Namibia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Namib_Desert_Namibia%282%29.jpg/250px-Namib_Desert_Namibia%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Namib_Desert_Namibia(2).jpg)

Sand dunes of the [Namib desert](/wiki/Namib_desert "Namib desert")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/SAC_Namibia-cactus.jpg/250px-SAC_Namibia-cactus.jpg)](/wiki/File:SAC_Namibia-cactus.jpg)

[Fish River Canyon](/wiki/Fish_River_Canyon "Fish River Canyon")

At 825,615 km2 (318,772 sq mi),[[86]](#cite_note-89) Namibia is the world's 34th largest country (after [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela "Venezuela")). It lies mostly between latitudes [17°](/wiki/17th_parallel_south "17th parallel south") and [29°S](/wiki/29th_parallel_south "29th parallel south") (a small area is north of 17°), and longitudes [11°](/wiki/11th_meridian_east "11th meridian east") and [26°E](/wiki/26th_meridian_east "26th meridian east").

The Namibian landscape consists generally of five geographical areas, each with characteristic [abiotic conditions](/wiki/Abiotic_component "Abiotic component") and vegetation, with some variation within and overlap between them: the Central Plateau, the [Namib](/wiki/Namib "Namib") Desert, the [Great Escarpment](/wiki/Great_Escarpment,_Southern_Africa "Great Escarpment, Southern Africa"), the [Bushveld](/wiki/Bushveld "Bushveld"), and the [Kalahari Desert](/wiki/Kalahari_Desert "Kalahari Desert").

Namibia is situated between the Namib and [Kalahari Deserts](/wiki/Kalahari_Desert "Kalahari Desert"). Namibia has the least rainfall of any country in sub-Saharan Africa.[[87]](#cite_note-90) The Namib is a broad expanse of hyper-arid gravel plains and dunes that stretches along Namibia's entire coastline. It varies between 100 and 200 km (60 and 120 mi) in width. Areas within the Namib include the Skeleton Coast and the [Kaokoveld](/wiki/Kaokoveld "Kaokoveld") in the north and the extensive Namib Sand Sea along the central coast.[[30]](#cite_note-at1315-33)

The Central Plateau runs from north to south, bordered by the [Skeleton Coast](/wiki/Skeleton_Coast "Skeleton Coast") (a coastal desert) to the northwest, the Namib Desert and its coastal plains to the southwest, the [Orange River](/wiki/Orange_River "Orange River") to the south, and the Kalahari Desert to the east. The Central Plateau is home to the highest point in Namibia at [Königstein](/wiki/K%C3%B6nigstein,_Namibia "Königstein, Namibia") elevation 2,573 m (8,440 ft; 1.60 mi).[[88]](#cite_note-elevation-91)[[89]](#cite_note-92)

The Great Escarpment swiftly rises to over 2,000 m (6,600 ft). Average temperatures and temperature ranges increase further inland from the cold Atlantic waters, while the lingering coastal fogs slowly diminish. Although the area is rocky with poorly developed soils, it is significantly more productive than the Namib Desert. As summer winds are forced over the Escarpment, moisture is extracted as precipitation.[[90]](#cite_note-93)

The Bushveld is found in north-eastern Namibia along the Angolan border and in the Caprivi Strip. The area receives a significantly greater amount of precipitation than the rest of the country, averaging around 400 mm (16 in) per year. The area is generally flat and the soils sandy, limiting their ability to retain water and support agriculture.[[91]](#cite_note-Cowling,_S._2001-94)

The Kalahari Desert, an arid region that extends into South Africa and Botswana, is one of Namibia's well-known geographical features. The Kalahari, while popularly known as a desert, has a variety of localised environments, including some verdant and technically non-desert areas. The [Succulent Karoo](/wiki/Succulent_Karoo "Succulent Karoo") is home to over 5,000 species of plants, nearly half of them [endemic](/wiki/Endemism "Endemism"); approximately 10 percent of the world's [succulents](/wiki/Succulents "Succulents") are found in the Karoo.[[92]](#cite_note-95)[[93]](#cite_note-96) The reason behind this high productivity and endemism may be the relatively stable nature of precipitation.[[94]](#cite_note-97)

Namibia's Coastal Desert is one of the oldest deserts in the world. Its sand dunes, created by the strong onshore winds, are the highest in the world.[[95]](#cite_note-98) Because of the location of the shoreline, at the point where the Atlantic's cold water reaches Africa's hot climate, often extremely dense fog forms along the coast.[[96]](#cite_note-99) Near the coast there are areas where the dune-hummocks are vegetated.[[97]](#cite_note-100) Namibia has rich coastal and marine resources that remain largely unexplored.[[98]](#cite_note-Sparks-101) The [Caprivi Strip](/wiki/Caprivi_Strip "Caprivi Strip") extends east from the northeastern corner of the country.

### Urban settlements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Urban settlements")]

Main article: [List of cities and towns in Namibia](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_Namibia "List of cities and towns in Namibia")

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Climate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Koppen-Geiger_Map_NAM_present.svg/500px-Koppen-Geiger_Map_NAM_present.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Koppen-Geiger_Map_NAM_present.svg)

[Köppen climate types](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") of Namibia

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Namibia-1113.jpg/250px-Namibia-1113.jpg)](/wiki/File:Namibia-1113.jpg)

Namibia is primarily a large desert and a semi-desert plateau

Namibia extends from 17°S to 25°S latitude: climatically the range of the sub-Tropical High Pressure Belt. Its overall climate description is arid, descending from the Sub-Humid [mean rain above 500 mm (20 in)] through Semi-Arid [between 300 and 500 mm (12 and 20 in)] (embracing most of the waterless Kalahari) and Arid [from 150 to 300 mm (5.9 to 12 in)] (these three regions are inland from the western [escarpment](/wiki/Great_Escarpment,_Southern_Africa "Great Escarpment, Southern Africa")) to the Hyper-Arid coastal plain [less than 100 mm (3.9 in)]. Temperature maxima are limited by the overall elevation of the entire region: only in the far south, [Warmbad](/wiki/Warmbad,_Namibia "Warmbad, Namibia") for instance, are maxima above 40 °C (104 °F) recorded.[[102]](#cite_note-105)

Typically the sub-Tropical High Pressure Belt, with frequent clear skies, provides more than 300 days of sunshine per year. It is situated at the southern edge of the tropics; the [Tropic of Capricorn](/wiki/Tropic_of_Capricorn "Tropic of Capricorn") cuts the country about in half. The winter (June–August) is generally dry. Both rainy seasons occur in summer: the small rainy season between September and November, and the big one between February and April.[[103]](#cite_note-106) Humidity is low, and average rainfall varies from almost zero in the [Skeleton Coast](/wiki/Skeleton_Coast "Skeleton Coast") (a coastal desert) to more than 600 mm (24 in) in the Caprivi Strip. Rainfall is highly variable, and droughts are common.[[104]](#cite_note-EBClimate-107) In the summer of 2006–07 the rainfall was recorded far below the annual average.[[105]](#cite_note-Olszewski1-108) In May 2019, Namibia declared a state of emergency in response to the drought,[[106]](#cite_note-109) and extended it by an additional 6 months in October 2019.[[107]](#cite_note-110)

Weather and climate in the coastal area are dominated by the cold, north-flowing [Benguela Current](/wiki/Benguela_Current "Benguela Current") of the Atlantic Ocean, which accounts for very low [precipitation](/wiki/Precipitation_(meteorology) "Precipitation (meteorology)") (50 mm (2.0 in) per year or less), frequent dense fog, and overall lower temperatures than in the rest of the country.[[104]](#cite_note-EBClimate-107) In Winter, occasionally a condition known as **[Bergwind](/wiki/Bergwind "Bergwind")** (German for "mountain wind") or **Oosweer** ([Afrikaans](/wiki/Afrikaans "Afrikaans") for "east weather") occurs, a hot dry wind blowing from the inland to the coast. As the area behind the coast is a desert, these winds can develop into sand storms, leaving sand deposits in the Atlantic Ocean that are visible on satellite images.[[108]](#cite_note-Olszewski2-111)

The Central Plateau and Kalahari areas have wide [diurnal](/wiki/Diurnal_temperature_variation "Diurnal temperature variation") temperature ranges of up to 30C (54F).[[104]](#cite_note-EBClimate-107)

*Efundja*, the annual seasonal flooding of the northern parts of the country, often causes not only damage to infrastructure but loss of life.[[109]](#cite_note-112) The rains that cause these floods originate in Angola, flow into Namibia's [Cuvelai-Etosha Basin](/wiki/Cuvelai-Etosha_Basin "Cuvelai-Etosha Basin"), and fill the *oshanas* ([Oshiwambo](/wiki/Oshiwambo "Oshiwambo"): flood plains) there. The worst floods so far[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit) occurred in March 2011 and displaced 21,000 people.[[110]](#cite_note-113)

### Water sources

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Water sources")]

Main article: [Water supply and sanitation in Namibia](/wiki/Water_supply_and_sanitation_in_Namibia "Water supply and sanitation in Namibia")

Namibia is the driest country in [sub-Saharan Africa](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa") and depends largely on groundwater. With an average rainfall of about 350 mm (14 in) per annum, the highest rainfall occurs in the Caprivi Strip in the northeast (about 600 mm (24 in) per annum) and decreases in a westerly and southwesterly direction to as little as 50 mm (2.0 in) and less per annum at the coast. The only perennial rivers are found on the national borders with South Africa, Angola, Zambia, and the short border with Botswana in the Caprivi Strip. In the interior of the country, surface water is available only in the summer months when rivers are in flood after exceptional rainfalls. Otherwise, surface water is restricted to a few large storage dams retaining and damming up these seasonal floods and their run-off. Where people do not live near perennial rivers or make use of the storage dams, they are dependent on groundwater. Even isolated communities and those economic activities located far from good surface water sources, such as mining, agriculture, and tourism, can be supplied from groundwater over nearly 80% of the country.[[111]](#cite_note-114)

More than 100,000 [boreholes](/wiki/Borehole "Borehole") have been drilled in Namibia over the past century. One third of these boreholes have been drilled dry.[[112]](#cite_note-115) An [aquifer](/wiki/Aquifer "Aquifer") called Ohangwena II, on both sides of the Angola-Namibia border, was discovered in 2012. It has been estimated to be capable of supplying a population of 800,000 people in the North for 400 years, at the current (2018) rate of consumption.[[113]](#cite_note-aqui-116) Experts estimate that Namibia has 7,720 km3 (1,850 cu mi) of underground water.[[114]](#cite_note-afr-117)[[115]](#cite_note-aqmap-118)

On 8 June 2023, Namibia became the first Southern African country and the eighth country in Africa to accede to the Convention on the Protection and Use of Transboundary Watercourses and International Lakes (UN Water Convention).[[116]](#cite_note-119)

### Communal wildlife conservancies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Communal wildlife conservancies")]

Main article: [Communal wildlife conservancies in Namibia](/wiki/Communal_wildlife_conservancies_in_Namibia "Communal wildlife conservancies in Namibia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/SAC_Namibia-bushveld.jpg/330px-SAC_Namibia-bushveld.jpg)](/wiki/File:SAC_Namibia-bushveld.jpg)

[Quivertree](/wiki/Quivertree "Quivertree") Forest, [Bushveld](/wiki/Bushveld "Bushveld")

Namibia is one of few countries in the world to specifically address [conservation](/wiki/Wildlife_conservation "Wildlife conservation") and protection of [natural resources](/wiki/Natural_resources "Natural resources") in its constitution.[[117]](#cite_note-Stefanova-120) Article 95 states, "The State shall actively promote and maintain the welfare of the people by adopting international policies aimed at the following: maintenance of [ecosystems](/wiki/Ecology "Ecology"), essential ecological processes, and biological diversity of Namibia, and utilisation of living natural resources on a sustainable basis for the benefit of all Namibians, both present and future."[[117]](#cite_note-Stefanova-120)

In 1993, Namibia's newly formed government received funding from the [United States Agency for International Development](/wiki/United_States_Agency_for_International_Development "United States Agency for International Development") (USAID) through its Living in a Finite Environment (LIFE) Project.[[118]](#cite_note-121) The [Ministry of Environment and Tourism](/wiki/Ministry_of_Environment_and_Tourism_(Namibia) "Ministry of Environment and Tourism (Namibia)"), with financial support from organisations such as USAID, [Endangered Wildlife Trust](/wiki/Endangered_Wildlife_Trust "Endangered Wildlife Trust"), [World Wide Fund for Nature](/wiki/World_Wide_Fund_for_Nature "World Wide Fund for Nature"), and Canadian Ambassador's Fund, together form a Community-Based Natural Resource Management (CBNRM) support structure. The project's main goal is to promote sustainable natural resource management by giving local communities rights to wildlife management and tourism.[[119]](#cite_note-UNEP-122)

### Wildlife

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Wildlife")]

Main article: [Wildlife of Namibia](/wiki/Wildlife_of_Namibia "Wildlife of Namibia")

Namibia has various species of wildlife including the [wild dog](/wiki/African_wild_dog "African wild dog"), [dik dik](/wiki/Dik-dik "Dik-dik") and critically endangered [black rhino](/wiki/Black_rhinoceros "Black rhinoceros"). There are 200 terrestrial mammal species, 645 bird species and 115 fish species.[[120]](#cite_note-123)[[121]](#cite_note-124)

## Government and politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Government and politics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Tintenpalast-Windhoek.jpg/250px-Tintenpalast-Windhoek.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tintenpalast-Windhoek.jpg)

[Tintenpalast](/wiki/Tintenpalast "Tintenpalast"), the centre of Namibia's government

Main articles: [Politics of Namibia](/wiki/Politics_of_Namibia "Politics of Namibia") and [Elections in Namibia](/wiki/Elections_in_Namibia "Elections in Namibia")

Namibia is a unitary [semi-presidential](/wiki/Semi-presidential_system "Semi-presidential system") representative democratic [republic](/wiki/Republic "Republic").[[12]](#cite_note-Dual-14) The [President of Namibia](/wiki/President_of_Namibia "President of Namibia") is elected to a five-year term and is both the [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") and the [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government").[[122]](#cite_note-CN-125) All members of the government are individually and collectively responsible to the legislature.[[123]](#cite_note-126)[[124]](#cite_note-ECN-127)

The [Constitution of Namibia](/wiki/Constitution_of_Namibia "Constitution of Namibia") outlines the following as the organs of the country's government:[[125]](#cite_note-Bösl-128)

* Executive: executive power is exercised by the President and the [Government](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Namibia "Cabinet of Namibia").
* Legislature: Namibia has a [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameralism "Bicameralism") [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Namibia "Parliament of Namibia") with the [National Assembly](/wiki/National_Assembly_of_Namibia "National Assembly of Namibia") as [lower house](/wiki/Lower_house "Lower house"), and the [National Council](/wiki/National_Council_of_Namibia "National Council of Namibia") as the [upper house](/wiki/Upper_house "Upper house").[[126]](#cite_note-parliament153-129)
* [Judiciary](/wiki/Judiciary "Judiciary"): Namibia has a system of courts that interpret and apply the law in the name of the state.

While the constitution envisaged a [multi-party system](/wiki/Multi-party_system "Multi-party system") for Namibia's government, the [SWAPO](/wiki/SWAPO "SWAPO") party has been [dominant](/wiki/Dominant-party_system "Dominant-party system") since independence in 1990.[[127]](#cite_note-130) According to 2023 [V-Dem Democracy indices](/wiki/V-Dem_Democracy_indices "V-Dem Democracy indices") Namibia is ranked 66th electoral democracy worldwide and 8th [electoral democracy in Africa](/wiki/Democracy_in_Africa "Democracy in Africa").[[128]](#cite_note-vdem_dataset-131)

### Foreign relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Foreign relations")]

Main article: [Foreign relations of Namibia](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Namibia "Foreign relations of Namibia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Russia-Africa_Summit_in_Sochi_%282019-10-24%29.jpg/250px-Russia-Africa_Summit_in_Sochi_%282019-10-24%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Russia-Africa_Summit_in_Sochi_(2019-10-24).jpg)

Former President [Hage Geingob](/wiki/Hage_Geingob "Hage Geingob") (second row, first from the right) with other African leaders and Russian President [Vladimir Putin](/wiki/Vladimir_Putin "Vladimir Putin") at the [Russia–Africa Summit](/wiki/Russia%E2%80%93Africa_Summit_2019 "Russia–Africa Summit 2019") in Sochi, 24 October 2019

Namibia has a largely independent [foreign policy](/wiki/Foreign_policy "Foreign policy"), with persisting affiliations with states that aided the independence struggle, including [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba"). With a small army and a fragile economy, the Namibian government's principal foreign policy concern is developing strengthened ties within the Southern African region. A member of the [Southern African Development Community](/wiki/Southern_African_Development_Community "Southern African Development Community"), Namibia is a vocal advocate for greater regional integration. It became the 160th member of the UN on 23 April 1990. On its independence it became the 50th member of the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations").

### Military

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Military")]

Main article: [Namibian Defence Force](/wiki/Namibian_Defence_Force "Namibian Defence Force")

The 2019 budget for the [Ministry of Defence](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defence_(Namibia) "Ministry of Defence (Namibia)") was N$5,885 million (a 1.2% decrease from the previous financial year).[[129]](#cite_note-132) With close to 6 billion Namibian dollars (US$411 million in 2021) the Ministry of Defence receives the fourth highest amount of money from government per ministry.

Namibia does not have any enemies in [the region](/wiki/Southern_African_Development_Community "Southern African Development Community"), though it has been involved in various disputes regarding borders and construction plans.[[130]](#cite_note-133)

The Namibian constitution defines the role of the military as "*defending the territory and national interests.*" Namibia formed the [Namibian Defence Force](/wiki/Namibian_Defence_Force "Namibian Defence Force") (NDF), comprising former enemies in a 23-year bush war: the [People's Liberation Army of Namibia](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army_of_Namibia "People's Liberation Army of Namibia") (PLAN) and [South West African](/wiki/South_West_Africa "South West Africa") *Territorial Force* ([SWATF](/wiki/SWATF "SWATF")). The [British government](/wiki/British_government "British government") formulated the plan for integrating these forces and began training the NDF, which consists of a small headquarters and five battalions.

The [United Nations Transitional Assistance Group](/wiki/United_Nations_Transitional_Assistance_Group "United Nations Transitional Assistance Group") (UNTAG)'s Kenyan infantry battalion remained in Namibia for three months after independence to help train the NDF and to stabilise the north. According to the Namibian Defence Ministry, enlistments of both men and women will number no more than 7,500.

The chief of the Namibian Defence Force is Air Vice Marshal [Martin Kambulu Pinehas](/wiki/Martin_Pinehas "Martin Pinehas") (with effect from 1 April 2020).

In 2017, Namibia signed the UN [treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons](/wiki/Treaty_on_the_Prohibition_of_Nuclear_Weapons "Treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons").[[131]](#cite_note-134)

### Administrative divisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Administrative divisions")]

Main article: [Administrative divisions of Namibia](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Namibia "Administrative divisions of Namibia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Namibia_Population_Density_%282011%29.jpg/330px-Namibia_Population_Density_%282011%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Namibia_Population_Density_(2011).jpg)

Population density in Namibia by regions (census 2011)

Namibia is divided into 14 regions which are subdivided into 121 constituencies. The administrative division of Namibia is tabled by *Delimitation Commissions* and accepted or declined by the [National Assembly](/wiki/National_Assembly_of_Namibia "National Assembly of Namibia"). Since state foundation four Delimitation Commissions have delivered their work, the last one in 2013 under the chairmanship of Judge Alfred Siboleka.[[132]](#cite_note-135) The most urbanised and economically active regions are the Khomas and Erongo regions, with Khomas home to the capital, Windhoek, and Erongo home to Walvis Bay and Swakopmund.
