Regional councillors are directly elected through secret ballots (regional elections) by the inhabitants of their constituencies.[[133]](#cite_note-136)

Local authorities in Namibia can be in the form of municipalities (either Part 1 or Part 2 municipalities), town councils or villages.[[134]](#cite_note-alan-137)

### Human rights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Human rights")]

Main article: [Human rights in Namibia](/wiki/Human_rights_in_Namibia "Human rights in Namibia")

Namibia is one of the most free and democratic countries in Africa,[[135]](#cite_note-138) with a government that maintains and protects human rights and freedoms. Namibia was ranked first on mainland Africa in the [Human Freedom Index](/wiki/Human_Freedom_Index "Human Freedom Index") 2025, bettered only by the three island nations of Cape Verde, Mauritius, and Seychelles.[[136]](#cite_note-139) However, significant issues include government corruption, policy inertia and prison overcrowding. Also, refugees are not permitted free movement.[[137]](#cite_note-USDOS-140)

Homosexual acts were formerly illegal in Namibia,[[138]](#cite_note-:4-141) although the respective law was not enforced.[[139]](#cite_note-142) Discrimination, as well as intolerance, against [LGBT people](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_Namibia "LGBT rights in Namibia") is widespread, specifically in rural areas. Urban areas are generally neutral or supportive with a few LGBT-dedicated clubs and events.[[140]](#cite_note-143) Some Namibian government officials and high-profile figures, such as Namibia's [Ombudsman](/wiki/Ombudsman "Ombudsman") John Walters and First Lady [Monica Geingos](/wiki/Monica_Geingos "Monica Geingos"), had called for [sodomy](/wiki/Sodomy "Sodomy") and homosexuality to be decriminalised and are in favour of [LGBT rights](/wiki/LGBT_rights "LGBT rights").[[138]](#cite_note-:4-141)[[141]](#cite_note-144) In 2023, the Supreme Court ruled that same-sex marriages legally made outside of Namibia must be recognized by the government.[[142]](#cite_note-145) In 2024 the Windhoek High Court ruled the ban on homosexual acts between men to be unconstitutional.[[143]](#cite_note-146)

In November 2018, it was reported that 32% of women aged 15–49 experienced [violence](/wiki/Violence_against_women "Violence against women") and [domestic abuse](/wiki/Domestic_abuse "Domestic abuse") from their spouses/partners and 29.5% of men believe that physical abuse towards their wife/partner is acceptable, although this is typically in rural areas.[[144]](#cite_note-147) The Namibian constitution guarantees the rights, freedoms and equal treatment of women in Namibia[[145]](#cite_note-148) and SWAPO, the ruling party in Namibia, has adopted a "zebra system", which ensures a fair balance of both genders in government and equal representation of women in the Namibian government.[[146]](#cite_note-149)[[137]](#cite_note-USDOS-140)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economy of Namibia](/wiki/Economy_of_Namibia "Economy of Namibia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Namibia_GDP_per_capita.png/250px-Namibia_GDP_per_capita.png)](/wiki/File:Namibia_GDP_per_capita.png)

Namibia GDP per capita, 2000–2022

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Downtown_Windhoek%2C_Independence_Avenue.jpg/250px-Downtown_Windhoek%2C_Independence_Avenue.jpg)](/wiki/File:Downtown_Windhoek,_Independence_Avenue.jpg)

Downtown [Windhoek](/wiki/Windhoek "Windhoek")

The trans-African automobile route – the [Tripoli-Cape Town Highway](/wiki/Tripoli-Cape_Town_Highway "Tripoli-Cape Town Highway") and the [Trans-Kalahari Corridor](/wiki/Trans-Kalahari_Corridor "Trans-Kalahari Corridor") pass through Namibia. Namibia's economy is tied closely to [South Africa](/wiki/Economy_of_South_Africa "Economy of South Africa")'s due to their shared history.[[147]](#cite_note-Intelligence1-150)[[148]](#cite_note-Department1-151) In Q3 2023, the largest economic sectors were mining (18.0% of GDP), public administration (12.9%), manufacturing (10.1%), and education (9.2%).[[149]](#cite_note-152)

Namibia has a highly developed banking and financial services sector with modern infrastructures, such as online banking and cellphone banking. The [Bank of Namibia](/wiki/Bank_of_Namibia "Bank of Namibia") (BoN) is the central bank of Namibia responsible for performing all other functions ordinarily performed by a central bank. There are five BoN authorised commercial banks in Namibia: Bank Windhoek, First National Bank, Nedbank, Standard Bank and Small and Medium Enterprises Bank.[[150]](#cite_note-153) Namibia's economy is characterised by a divide between the formal and the informal economies, which is in part aggravated by the legacy of apartheid spatial planning.[[151]](#cite_note-154)

The country's unemployment rate was 33.4% in 2018, with a labour force of 1,090,153.[[152]](#cite_note-155) As of 2023, the country has a youth unemployment rate of 38.4%,[[153]](#cite_note-156) one of the highest in the world. However, Namibia has a high percentage of skilled labour relative to SADC countries and have relatively low unemployment rates for skilled workers. To fight high unemployment, particularly amongst the youth, the government approved the introduction of an Internship Tax Incentive Programme aimed at incentivising employers to enroll more interns by providing an additional corporate tax deduction. The total financial implication for the Government is estimated at N$126 million.

[Poverty](/wiki/Poverty_in_Namibia "Poverty in Namibia") and inequality remain significant in the country. 40.9% of the population is affected by [multidimensional poverty](/wiki/Multidimensional_Poverty_Index "Multidimensional Poverty Index") while an additional 19.2 percent is classified as vulnerable to multidimensional poverty.[[26]](#cite_note-:5-29) [Income disparity](/wiki/Income_disparity "Income disparity") in the country remains one of the world's highest with a [Gini coefficient](/wiki/Gini_coefficient "Gini coefficient") of 59.1 in 2015.[[28]](#cite_note-:6-31)

In 2004 a labour act was passed to protect people from job discrimination stemming from pregnancy and HIV/AIDS status. In early 2010 the Government [tender board](/wiki/Tender_board "Tender board") announced that "henceforth 100 per cent of all unskilled and semi-skilled labour must be sourced, without exception, from within Namibia".[[154]](#cite_note-157)

In 2013, global business and financial news provider [Bloomberg](/wiki/Bloomberg_L.P. "Bloomberg L.P.") named Namibia the top emerging market economy in Africa and the 13th best in the world. Only four African countries made the Top 20 Emerging Markets list in the March 2013 issue of Bloomberg Markets magazine, and Namibia was rated ahead of [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco") (19th), South Africa (15th), and Zambia (14th). Worldwide, Namibia also fared better than Hungary, Brazil, and Mexico. Bloomberg Markets magazine ranked the top 20 based on more than a dozen criteria. The data came from Bloomberg's own financial-market statistics, IMF forecasts and the World Bank. The countries were also rated on areas of particular interest to foreign investors: the ease of doing business, the perceived level of corruption and economic freedom. To attract foreign investment, the government has made improvement in reducing red tape resulted from excessive government regulations, making Namibia one of the least bureaucratic places to do business in the region. Facilitation payments are occasionally demanded by customs due to cumbersome and costly customs procedures.[[155]](#cite_note-158) Namibia is also classified as an Upper Middle Income country by the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank"), and ranks 87th out of 185 economies in terms of ease of doing business.[[156]](#cite_note-159)

The [cost of living in Namibia](/wiki/Cost_of_living_in_Namibia "Cost of living in Namibia") is slightly above average because most goods, including cereals, need to be imported. Its capital city, Windhoek, is the 150th most expensive place in the world for expatriates to live.[[157]](#cite_note-160)

[Taxation in Namibia](/wiki/Taxation_in_Namibia "Taxation in Namibia") includes personal income tax, which is applicable to the total taxable income of an individual. All individuals are taxed at progressive marginal rates over a series of income brackets. Tax in Namibia is less than South African tax at monthly incomes greater than N$58,754, with the country's effective tax rates typically plateauing at a maximum of 30.8% while South Africa's plateaus at 37.4%.[[158]](#cite_note-161) This makes it favourable for wealthy South Africans to migrate to Namibia given their similar cost of living, cultures and socio-economic factors. In 2024, the government announced in its FY 2024/25 Budget Statement that personal income tax would be lowered, increasing the minimum taxable income from N$50,000 to N$100,000 and reducing taxable income in higher brackets as well.[[159]](#cite_note-FY202425-162)

The value-added tax (VAT) is applicable to most of the commodities and services, except for staple goods such as bread.[[160]](#cite_note-163)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Sandstorm_while_driving_from_Swakopmund_to_Walfish_Bay%2C_2005.jpg/250px-Sandstorm_while_driving_from_Swakopmund_to_Walfish_Bay%2C_2005.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sandstorm_while_driving_from_Swakopmund_to_Walfish_Bay,_2005.jpg)

The [B2](/wiki/B2_road_(Namibia) "B2 road (Namibia)") between [Swakopmund](/wiki/Swakopmund "Swakopmund") and [Walvis Bay](/wiki/Walvis_Bay "Walvis Bay"), Namibia

Despite the remote nature of much of the country, Namibia has seaports, airports, highways, well-maintained roads, infrastructure and [railways](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Namibia "Rail transport in Namibia") (narrow-gauge). It is an important regional transportation hub for its seaports and trade with landlocked neighbouring countries. The Central Plateau already serves as a [transportation corridor](/wiki/Transportation_corridor "Transportation corridor") from the more densely populated north to South Africa, the source of four-fifths of Namibia's imports.[[161]](#cite_note-World_Almanac_2004-164)

### Agriculture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Agriculture")]

Main article: [Agriculture in Namibia](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Namibia "Agriculture in Namibia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Schildburgsdorf.jpg/250px-Schildburgsdorf.jpg)](/wiki/File:Schildburgsdorf.jpg)

Welcoming sign of the *Burgsdorf* farm in [Hardap](/wiki/Hardap_Region "Hardap Region")

About half of the population depends on agriculture (largely [subsistence agriculture](/wiki/Subsistence_agriculture "Subsistence agriculture")) for its livelihood, but Namibia must still import some of its food. Although per capita GDP is five times the per capita GDP of Africa's poorest countries, the majority of Namibia's people live in rural areas and have a subsistence way of life. Namibia has one of the [highest rates of income inequality](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_income_equality "List of countries by income equality") in the world, due in part to the fact that there is an urban economy and a more rural cashless economy. The inequality figures thus take into account people who do not actually rely on the formal economy for their survival. Although arable land accounts for <1% of Namibia, (about .97%), nearly half of the population is employed in agriculture.[[161]](#cite_note-World_Almanac_2004-164)

About 4,000 commercial farmers own almost half of Namibia's arable land.[[162]](#cite_note-165) The United Kingdom offered about $180,000 in 2004 to help finance Namibia's [land reform](/wiki/Land_reform "Land reform") process, as Namibia plans to start expropriating land from white farmers to resettle landless black Namibians.[[163]](#cite_note-166) Germany has offered €1.1bn in 2021 over 30 years in reparations for the genocides in the early 20th century but the money will go towards infrastructure, healthcare and training programmes not land reform.[[164]](#cite_note-167)

An agreement has been reached on the [privatisation](/wiki/Privatisation "Privatisation") of several more enterprises in coming years, with hopes that this will stimulate much needed foreign investment, but reinvestment of environmentally derived capital has hobbled Namibian per capita income.[[165]](#cite_note-Lange-168) One of the fastest growing areas of economic development in Namibia is the growth of [wildlife conservancies](/wiki/Communal_Wildlife_Conservancies_in_Namibia "Communal Wildlife Conservancies in Namibia").

### Mining and electricity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Mining and electricity")]

Main article: [Mining in Namibia](/wiki/Mining_in_Namibia "Mining in Namibia")

Providing 25% of Namibia's revenue, mining is the single most important contributor to the economy.[[166]](#cite_note-169) Namibia is the fourth largest exporter of non-fuel minerals in Africa and was the world's fourth largest producer of [uranium](/wiki/Uranium "Uranium"). There have been significant investment in [uranium mining](/wiki/Uranium_mining "Uranium mining") and Namibia planned to become the largest exporter of uranium by 2015.[[167]](#cite_note-170) However, as of 2019 Namibia continued to produce 750 tons of uranium annually making it a smaller than average exporter in the competitive world market.[[168]](#cite_note-171) Rich [alluvial](/wiki/Alluvium "Alluvium") diamond deposits make Namibia a primary source for gem-quality diamonds.[[169]](#cite_note-172) While Namibia is known predominantly for its gem diamond and uranium deposits, a number of other minerals are extracted industrially such as [lead](/wiki/Lead "Lead"), [tungsten](/wiki/Tungsten "Tungsten"), [gold](/wiki/Gold "Gold"), [tin](/wiki/Tin "Tin"), [fluorspar](/wiki/Fluorspar "Fluorspar"), [manganese](/wiki/Manganese "Manganese"), [marble](/wiki/Marble "Marble"), [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper") and [zinc](/wiki/Zinc "Zinc"). Country's gold production in 2015 was 6 metric tons.[[170]](#cite_note-173) There are offshore gas deposits in the Atlantic Ocean that are planned to be extracted in the future.[[171]](#cite_note-usds-174) According to "The Diamond Investigation", a book about the global diamond market, from 1978, [De Beers](/wiki/De_Beers "De Beers"), the largest diamond company, bought most of the Namibian diamonds, and would continue to do so, because "whatever government eventually comes to power they will need this revenue to survive".[[172]](#cite_note-175)

Although much of the world's diamond supply comes from what have been called African [blood diamonds](/wiki/Blood_diamonds "Blood diamonds"), Namibia has managed to develop a diamond mining industry largely free of the kinds of conflict, extortion, and murder that have plagued many other African nations with diamond mines. This has been attributed to political dynamics, economic institutions, grievances, political geography, and the effects of neighbourhoods, and is the result of a joint agreement between the government and [De Beers](/wiki/De_Beers "De Beers") that has led to a taxable base, strengthening state institutions.[[173]](#cite_note-176)

Estimates updated in 2022 suggest that two exploration wells in the offshore Orange Basin could hold 2 and 3 billion barrels of oil, respectively. The expected revenue could transform Namibia's domestic economy and facilitate sustainable development goals.[[174]](#cite_note-bra1-177)

Domestic supply voltage is 220 V AC. Electricity is generated mainly by thermal and hydroelectric power plants. Non-conventional methods of electricity generation also play some role. Encouraged by the rich uranium deposits, in 2010 the Namibian government planned to erect its first nuclear power station by 2018. Uranium enrichment was also envisaged to take place locally.[[175]](#cite_note-Saveorsink-178)

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Tourism")]

Main article: [Tourism in Namibia](/wiki/Tourism_in_Namibia "Tourism in Namibia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Equus_burchelli_4.jpg/250px-Equus_burchelli_4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Equus_burchelli_4.jpg)

An example of Namibian wildlife, the [plains zebra](/wiki/Plains_zebra "Plains zebra"), is one focus of tourism.

Tourism is a major contributor (14.5%) to Namibia's GDP, creating tens of thousands of jobs (18.2% of all employment) directly or indirectly and servicing over a million tourists per year.[[176]](#cite_note-179) The country is a prime destination in Africa and is known for [ecotourism](/wiki/Ecotourism "Ecotourism"), which features [Namibia's extensive wildlife](/wiki/Wildlife_of_Namibia "Wildlife of Namibia").[[177]](#cite_note-goodshape-180)

There are many lodges and reserves to accommodate ecotourists. Sport and [trophy hunting](/wiki/Trophy_hunting "Trophy hunting") is also a large and growing component of the Namibian economy, accounting for 14% of total tourism in the year 2000, or 19.6 million U.S. dollars, with Namibia boasting numerous species sought after by international sport hunters.[[178]](#cite_note-Humavindu-181)

In addition, extreme sports such as [sandboarding](/wiki/Sandboarding "Sandboarding"), [skydiving](/wiki/Skydiving "Skydiving") and 4x4ing have become popular, and many cities have companies that provide tours.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The most visited places include the capital city of [Windhoek](/wiki/Windhoek "Windhoek"), [Caprivi Strip](/wiki/Caprivi_Strip "Caprivi Strip"), [Fish River Canyon](/wiki/Fish_River_Canyon "Fish River Canyon"), [Sossusvlei](/wiki/Sossusvlei "Sossusvlei"), the [Skeleton Coast](/wiki/Skeleton_Coast "Skeleton Coast") Park, [Sesriem](/wiki/Sesriem "Sesriem"), [Etosha Pan](/wiki/Etosha_Pan "Etosha Pan") and the coastal towns of [Swakopmund](/wiki/Swakopmund "Swakopmund"), [Walvis Bay](/wiki/Walvis_Bay "Walvis Bay") and [Lüderitz](/wiki/L%C3%BCderitz "Lüderitz").[[179]](#cite_note-182)

Windhoek plays a very important role in Namibia's tourism due to its central location and close proximity to [Hosea Kutako International Airport](/wiki/Hosea_Kutako_International_Airport "Hosea Kutako International Airport"). According to The Namibia Tourism Exit Survey, which was produced by the [Millennium Challenge Corporation](/wiki/Millennium_Challenge_Corporation "Millennium Challenge Corporation") for the Namibian Directorate of Tourism, 56% of all tourists visiting Namibia in 2012–13 visited Windhoek.[[180]](#cite_note-183) Many of Namibia's tourism related parastatals and governing bodies such as Namibia Wildlife Resorts and the [Namibia Tourism Board](/wiki/Namibia_Tourism_Board "Namibia Tourism Board") as well as Namibia's tourism-related [trade associations](/wiki/Trade_association "Trade association") such as the [Hospitality Association of Namibia](/wiki/Hospitality_Association_of_Namibia "Hospitality Association of Namibia") are headquartered in Windhoek.[[181]](#cite_note-184) There are also a number of notable hotels in Windhoek, such as [Windhoek Country Club Resort](/wiki/Windhoek_Country_Club_Resort "Windhoek Country Club Resort"), and some international hotel chains, such as [Hilton Hotels and Resorts](/wiki/Hilton_Hotels_%26_Resorts "Hilton Hotels & Resorts").

Namibia's primary tourism related governing body, the [Namibia Tourism Board](/wiki/Namibia_Tourism_Board "Namibia Tourism Board") (NTB), was established by an Act of Parliament: the *Namibia Tourism Board Act, 2000* (Act 21 of 2000). Its primary objectives are to regulate the tourism industry and to market Namibia as a tourist destination.[[182]](#cite_note-185) There are also a number of [trade associations](/wiki/Trade_association "Trade association") that represent the tourism sector in Namibia, such as the Federation of Namibia Tourism Associations (the umbrella body for all tourism associations in Namibia), the [Hospitality Association of Namibia](/wiki/Hospitality_Association_of_Namibia "Hospitality Association of Namibia"), the Association of Namibian Travel Agents, Car Rental Association of Namibia and the Tour and Safari Association of Namibia.[[183]](#cite_note-186)

### Water supply and sanitation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Water supply and sanitation")]

Main article: [Water supply and sanitation in Namibia](/wiki/Water_supply_and_sanitation_in_Namibia "Water supply and sanitation in Namibia")

The only bulk water supplier in Namibia is [NamWater](/wiki/NamWater "NamWater"), which sells it to the respective municipalities which in turn deliver it through their reticulation networks.[[184]](#cite_note-:0-187) In rural areas, the directorate of Rural Water Supply in the [Ministry of Agriculture, Water and Land Reform](/wiki/Ministry_of_Agriculture_(Namibia) "Ministry of Agriculture (Namibia)") is in charge of drinking water supply.[[184]](#cite_note-:0-187)

The [UN](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") evaluated in 2011 that Namibia has improved its water access network significantly since independence in 1990. A large part of the population can not, however, make use of these resources due to the prohibitively high consumption cost and the long distance between residences and water points in rural areas.[[184]](#cite_note-:0-187) As a result, many Namibians prefer the traditional wells over the available water points far away.[[185]](#cite_note-:1-188)

Compared to the efforts made to improve access to safe water, Namibia is lagging behind in the provision of adequate sanitation.[[186]](#cite_note-189) This includes 298 schools that have no toilet facilities.[[187]](#cite_note-:2-190) Over 50% of child deaths are related to lack of water, sanitation, or hygiene; 23% are due to diarrhea alone. The UN has identified a "sanitation crisis" in the country.[[185]](#cite_note-:1-188)

Apart from residences for upper and middle class households, sanitation is insufficient in most residential areas. Private flush toilets are too expensive for virtually all residents in [townships](/wiki/Township "Township") due to their water consumption and installation cost. As a result, access to [improved sanitation](/wiki/Improved_sanitation "Improved sanitation") has not increased much since independence: in Namibia's rural areas 13% of the population had more than basic sanitation, up from 8% in 1990. Many of Namibia's inhabitants have to resort to "flying toilets", plastic bags to defecate into, which after use are flung into the bush.[[188]](#cite_note-191) The use of open areas close to residential land for urination and defecation is very common[[189]](#cite_note-192) and has been identified as a major [health hazard](/wiki/Health_in_Namibia "Health in Namibia").[[187]](#cite_note-:2-190)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Demographics")]

Main article: [Demographics of Namibia](/wiki/Demographics_of_Namibia "Demographics of Namibia")

Namibia has the second-[lowest population density](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_population_density "List of countries by population density") of any sovereign country, after [Mongolia](/wiki/Mongolia "Mongolia"), as well as having the lowest population density of any sovereign country with a coastline.[[190]](#cite_note-unpop-193) In 2017 there were on average 3.08 people per km2.[[191]](#cite_note-194) The [total fertility rate](/wiki/Total_fertility_rate "Total fertility rate") in 2015 was 3.47 children per woman according to the UN which is lower than the average TFR in sub-Saharan Africa of 4.7.[[192]](#cite_note-195)

Namibia conducts a census every ten years. After independence the first Population and Housing Census was carried out in 1991; further rounds followed in 2001, 2011, and 2023 (delayed two years due to the COVID-19 pandemic and financial constraints).[[193]](#cite_note-2001Census-196) The data collection method is to count every person resident in Namibia on the census reference night, wherever they happen to be. This is called the *de facto* method.[[194]](#cite_note-197) For enumeration purposes the country is demarcated into 4,042 *enumeration areas*. These areas do not overlap with constituency boundaries to get reliable data for election purposes as well.[[195]](#cite_note-198)

The 2011 Population and Housing Census counted 2,113,077 inhabitants. Between 2001 and 2011 the annual population growth was 1.4%, down from 2.6% in the previous ten-year period.[[196]](#cite_note-snapshot-199) In 2023, the [Namibia Statistics Agency](/wiki/Namibia_Statistics_Agency "Namibia Statistics Agency") conducted another census, which counted 3,022,401 inhabitants.[[197]](#cite_note-200)

### Ethnic groups

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Ethnic groups")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Nama_Woman_Smoking_Kalahari_Desert_Namibia_Luca_Galuzzi_2004.JPG/250px-Nama_Woman_Smoking_Kalahari_Desert_Namibia_Luca_Galuzzi_2004.JPG)](/wiki/File:Nama_Woman_Smoking_Kalahari_Desert_Namibia_Luca_Galuzzi_2004.JPG)

A [Nama](/wiki/Nama_people "Nama people") woman

Namibia has many ethnic groups.[[10]](#cite_note-Census2023-10) The majority of the Namibian population is made of [Bantu](/wiki/Bantu_peoples "Bantu peoples") and [Khoisan](/wiki/Khoisan "Khoisan") peoples. The Bantu groups include the [Owambo](/wiki/Owambo_people "Owambo people"), [Herero](/wiki/Herero_people "Herero people"), [Kavango](/wiki/Kavango_people "Kavango people"), [Lozi](/wiki/Lozi_people "Lozi people"), [Tswana](/wiki/Tswana_people "Tswana people") and [Himba](/wiki/Himba_people "Himba people") peoples. The Khoisan groups encompass the [Damara](/wiki/Damara_people "Damara people"), [Nama](/wiki/Nama_people "Nama people"), and [San](/wiki/San_people "San people") peoples. There is also a mixed ancestry population consisting of [Coloureds](/wiki/Coloured_people_in_Namibia "Coloured people in Namibia") (2.1%) and [Basters](/wiki/Basters "Basters") (1.5%).[[10]](#cite_note-Census2023-10) There is a substantial [Chinese minority in Namibia](/wiki/Chinese_people_in_Namibia "Chinese people in Namibia"); it stood at 40,000 in 2006.[[198]](#cite_note-201)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Himba_Woman_and_Family.JPG/250px-Himba_Woman_and_Family.JPG)](/wiki/File:Himba_Woman_and_Family.JPG)

[Himba people](/wiki/Himba_people "Himba people") in northern Namibia

[Whites](/wiki/White_Namibians "White Namibians") (being mainly of [Afrikaner](/wiki/Afrikaner_people "Afrikaner people"), German, British and [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_diaspora "Portuguese diaspora") origin) make up 1.8% of the population. Although their proportion of the population decreased after [independence](/wiki/Independence_of_Namibia "Independence of Namibia") due to emigration and lower birth rates, they still form the second-largest population of [European ancestry](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Europe "Ethnic groups in Europe"), both in terms of percentage and actual numbers, in [Sub-Saharan Africa](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa") (after South Africa).[[10]](#cite_note-Census2023-10) The majority of [Namibian whites](/wiki/White_Namibians "White Namibians") and nearly all those who are of [mixed race](/wiki/Basters "Basters") speak [Afrikaans](/wiki/Afrikaans "Afrikaans") and share similar origins, culture, and religion as the white and coloured populations of South Africa. A large minority of whites (around 30,000) trace their family origins back to the [German](/wiki/German_Namibians "German Namibians") settlers who colonised Namibia prior to the South African invasion during the First World War, and they maintain German cultural and educational institutions. Nearly all Portuguese settlers came to the country from the former [Portuguese colony](/wiki/Portuguese_West_Africa "Portuguese West Africa") of Angola.[[199]](#cite_note-202) The 1960 census reported 526,004 persons in what was then South West Africa, including 73,464 whites (14%).[[200]](#cite_note-203)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/%22Kinako_kakado%22.png/250px-%22Kinako_kakado%22.png)](/wiki/File:%22Kinako_kakado%22.png)

[Oshiwambo](/wiki/Ovambo_people "Ovambo people") women

### Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/42817_06.JPG/250px-42817_06.JPG)](/wiki/File:42817_06.JPG)

Secondary school students

Main article: [Education in Namibia](/wiki/Education_in_Namibia "Education in Namibia")

See also: [List of schools in Namibia](/wiki/List_of_schools_in_Namibia "List of schools in Namibia")

Namibia has free education for both primary and secondary education levels. Grades 1–7 are primary level, grades 8–12 are secondary. In 1998, there were 400,325 Namibian students in primary school and 115,237 students in secondary schools. The pupil-teacher ratio in 1999 was estimated at 32:1, with about 8% of the GDP being spent on education. Curriculum development, educational research, and professional development of teachers is centrally organised by the National Institute for Educational Development (NIED) in Okahandja.[[201]](#cite_note-204) Among sub-Saharan African countries, Namibia has one of the highest literacy rates.[[202]](#cite_note-auto-205) According to [CIA World Factbook](/wiki/CIA_World_Factbook "CIA World Factbook"), as of 2018 91.5% of the population age 15 and over can read and write.[[202]](#cite_note-auto-205)

Most schools in Namibia are state-run, but there are some private schools, which are also part of the country's education system. There are four teacher training universities, three colleges of agriculture, a police training college, and three universities: [University of Namibia](/wiki/University_of_Namibia "University of Namibia") (UNAM), [International University of Management](/wiki/International_University_of_Management "International University of Management") (IUM) and [Namibia University of Science and Technology](/wiki/Namibia_University_of_Science_and_Technology "Namibia University of Science and Technology") (NUST). Namibia was ranked 91st in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025.[[203]](#cite_note-206)[[204]](#cite_note-207)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Religion")]

Main article: [Religion in Namibia](/wiki/Religion_in_Namibia "Religion in Namibia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Swakopmund_ev-luth_Kirche_1.jpg/250px-Swakopmund_ev-luth_Kirche_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Swakopmund_ev-luth_Kirche_1.jpg)

Lutheran church in [Swakopmund](/wiki/Swakopmund "Swakopmund")

The Christian community makes up 80%–90% of the population of Namibia, with at least 75% being [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant"), of which at least 50% are [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"). Lutherans are the largest religious group, a legacy of the German and [Finnish](/wiki/Finland "Finland") missionary work during the country's colonial times.[[38]](#cite_note-finnish-mission-41) 10%–20% of the population hold [indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples "Indigenous peoples") beliefs.[[205]](#cite_note-CIA-208)

Missionary activities during the second half of the 19th century resulted in many Namibians converting to Christianity. Today most Christians are [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran"), but there also are [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic"), [Methodist](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism"), [Anglican](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism"), [African Methodist Episcopal](/wiki/African_Methodist_Episcopal_Church "African Methodist Episcopal Church"), and [Dutch Reformed](/wiki/Dutch_Reformed_Church "Dutch Reformed Church").

[Islam in Namibia](/wiki/Islam_in_Namibia "Islam in Namibia") is subscribed to by about 9,000 people,[[206]](#cite_note-209) many of them Nama.[[207]](#cite_note-210) Namibia is home to a small [Jewish](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") [community](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Namibia "History of the Jews in Namibia") of about 100 people.[[208]](#cite_note-jewishvirtuallibrary1-211)

Groups such as the [Latter-day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints") and [Jehovah's Witnesses](/wiki/Jehovah%27s_Witnesses "Jehovah's Witnesses") are also present in the country.

### Languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Languages")]

Main article: [Languages of Namibia](/wiki/Languages_of_Namibia "Languages of Namibia")

Home Languages in Namibia

|  |  |  |  |  |

The majority of Namibians can speak and understand English. Up to 1990, English, [German](/wiki/German_language_in_Namibia "German language in Namibia"), and [Afrikaans](/wiki/Afrikaans "Afrikaans") were official languages. Long before Namibia's independence from South Africa, SWAPO was of the opinion that the country should become officially monolingual, choosing this approach in contrast to that of its neighbour South Africa (which granted [all 12 of its major languages](/wiki/Languages_of_South_Africa "Languages of South Africa") official status), which it saw as "a deliberate policy of ethnolinguistic fragmentation."[[209]](#cite_note-212) Consequently, SWAPO instituted English as Namibia's sole official language, though only 2.3% of the population speaks it as a home language, but understood by most generally. Its implementation is focused on the civil service, education and the broadcasting system, especially the state broadcaster NBC.[[210]](#cite_note-kriger-213) Some other languages have received semi-official recognition by being allowed as medium of instruction in primary schools. Private schools are expected to follow the same policy as state schools, and "English language" is a compulsory subject.[[210]](#cite_note-kriger-213) Some critics argue that, as in other postcolonial African societies, the push for monolingual instruction and policy has resulted in a high rate of school drop-outs and of individuals whose academic competence in any language is low.[[211]](#cite_note-214)

According to the latest statistical data gathered in the most recent survey (2016), the linguistic landscape in the region has witnessed notable shifts since the 2011 census. [Oshiwambo](/wiki/Ovambo_language "Ovambo language") remains the predominant language, claiming the position of the most spoken language for a significant 49.7% of households, surpassing its previous standing. [KhoeKhoegowab](/wiki/KhoeKhoegowab "KhoeKhoegowab") follows at 11.0%, while the [Kavango](/wiki/Kavango_%E2%80%93_Southwest_Bantu_languages "Kavango – Southwest Bantu languages") Languages, with a share of 10.4%, have also experienced a noteworthy increase. Afrikaans, identified with a presence at 9.4%. The Herero Languages account for 9.2%, indicating a slight adjustment from the previous census. [Silozi](/wiki/Lozi_language "Lozi language") has seen a shift to 4.9%, and English, utilized primarily as a second language, stands at 2.3%. Other Languages collectively represent 1.0%, with San Languages at 0.7%, and German at 0.6%. The diversity of languages in the region is further demonstrated by the presence of Other African Languages at 0.5%, [Setswana](/wiki/Tswana_language "Tswana language") at 0.3%, and Other European Languages at 0.1%.[[212]](#cite_note-215)

Note: (1) Herero languages include: Otjiherero, Otjimbanderu, Oruzemba, Otjizimba, Otjihakahona, Otjindongona and Otjitjavikwa

(2) Kavango languages include: Rukwangali, Rushambyu, Rugciriku, Thimbukushu, Rumanyo and Rukavango

Most of the white population speaks English, Afrikaans or German. As a home language, Afrikaans is spoken by 60% of the white community, German by 32%, English by 7% and Portuguese by 4–5%.[[205]](#cite_note-CIA-208) Geographical proximity to Portuguese-speaking Angola explains the relatively high number of [Portuguese speakers](/wiki/Portuguese_speaker "Portuguese speaker"); in 2011 these were estimated to number 100,000.[[213]](#cite_note-216)

### Health

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Health")]

Main article: [Health in Namibia](/wiki/Health_in_Namibia "Health in Namibia")

See also: [HIV/AIDS in Namibia](/wiki/HIV/AIDS_in_Namibia "HIV/AIDS in Namibia")

[Life expectancy](/wiki/Life_expectancy "Life expectancy") at birth is estimated to be 64 years in 2017 – among the lowest in the world.[[214]](#cite_note-217)

Namibia launched a National Health Extension Programme in 2012[[215]](#cite_note-218) deployment 1,800 (2015) of a total ceiling of 4,800 health extension workers trained for six months in community health activities including first aid, health promotion for disease prevention, nutritional assessment and counseling, water sanitation and hygiene practices, HIV testing and community-based antiretroviral treatment.[[216]](#cite_note-219)

Namibia faces a non-communicable disease burden. The Demographic and Health Survey (2013) summarises findings on elevated blood pressure, hypertension, diabetes, and obesity:

* Among eligible respondents age 35–64, 44% of women and 45% of men have elevated blood pressure or are currently taking medicine to lower their blood pressure.
* 49% of women and 61% of men are not aware that they have elevated blood pressure.
* 43% of women and 34% of men with hypertension are taking medication for their condition.
* Only 29% of women and 20% of men with hypertension are taking medication and have their blood pressure under control.
* 6% of women and 7% of men are diabetic; that is, they have elevated fasting plasma glucose values or report that they are taking diabetes medication. An additional 7% of women and 6% of men are prediabetic.
* 67% of women and 74% of men with diabetes are taking medication to lower their blood glucose.
* Women and men with a higher-than-normal body mass index (25.0 or higher) are more likely to have elevated blood pressure and elevated fasting blood glucose.[[11]](#cite_note-:3-12): 237

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/AIDS_and_HIV_prevalence_2008.svg/330px-AIDS_and_HIV_prevalence_2008.svg.png)](/wiki/File:AIDS_and_HIV_prevalence_2008.svg)

Estimated percentage of HIV among young adults (15–49) per country as of 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit):[[217]](#cite_note-220)

|  |
| --- |
| 15–50 |

The [HIV epidemic](/wiki/HIV/AIDS_in_Namibia "HIV/AIDS in Namibia") remains a public health issue in Namibia despite significant achievements made by the [Ministry of Health and Social Services](/wiki/Ministry_of_Health_and_Social_Services_(Namibia) "Ministry of Health and Social Services (Namibia)") to expand HIV treatment services.[[218]](#cite_note-221) In 2001, there were an estimated 210,000 people living with HIV/AIDS, and the estimated death toll in 2003 was 16,000. According to the 2011 [UNAIDS](/wiki/UNAIDS "UNAIDS") Report, the epidemic in Namibia "appears to be leveling off."[[219]](#cite_note-222) As the HIV/AIDS epidemic has reduced the working-aged population, the number of orphans has increased. It falls to the government to provide education, food, shelter and clothing for these orphans.[[220]](#cite_note-223) A Demographic and Health Survey with an HIV biomarker was completed in 2013 and served as the fourth comprehensive, national-level population and health survey conducted in Namibia as part of the global Demographic and Health Surveys (DHS) programme. The DHS observed important characteristics associated to the HIV epidemic:[[11]](#cite_note-:3-12): 169, 203, 218

* Overall, 26 percent of men age 15–49 and 32 percent of those age 50–64 have been circumcised. HIV prevalence for men age 15–49 is lower among circumcised (8.0 percent) than among uncircumcised men (11.9 percent). The pattern of lower HIV prevalence among circumcised than uncircumcised men is observed across most background characteristics. For each age group, circumcised men have lower HIV prevalence than those who are not circumcised; the difference is especially pronounced for men age 35–39 and 45–49 (11.7 percentage points each). The difference in HIV prevalence between uncircumcised and circumcised men is larger among urban than rural men (5.2 percentage points versus 2.1 percentage points).
* HIV prevalence among respondents age 15–49 is 16.9 percent for women and 10.9 percent for men. HIV prevalence rates among women and men age 50–64 are similar (16.7 percent and 16.0 percent, respectively).
* HIV prevalence peaks in the 35–39 age group for both women and men (30.9 percent and 22.6 percent, respectively). It is lowest among respondents age 15–24 (2.5–6.4 percent for women and 2.0–3.4 percent for men).
* Among respondents age 15–49, HIV prevalence is highest for women and men in Zambezi (30.9 percent and 15.9 percent, respectively) and lowest for women in Omaheke (6.9 percent) and men in Ohangwena (6.6 percent).
* In 76.4 percent of the 1,007 cohabiting couples who were tested for HIV in the 2013 NDHS, both partners were HIV negative; in 10.1 percent of the couples, both partners were HIV positive; and 13.5 percent of the couples were discordant (that is, one partner was infected with HIV and the other was not).

In 2015, the Ministry of Health and Social Services and UNAIDS produced a progress report in which UNAIDS projected HIV prevalence among 15-to-49-year-olds at 13.3% [12.2–14.5%] and an estimated 210,000 [200,000–230,000] living with HIV.[[221]](#cite_note-224)[[222]](#cite_note-225)

The [malaria](/wiki/Malaria "Malaria") problem seems to be compounded by the AIDS epidemic. Research has shown that in Namibia the risk of contracting malaria is 14.5% greater if a person is also infected with HIV. The risk of death from malaria is also raised by approximately 50% with a concurrent HIV infection.[[223]](#cite_note-Korenromp-226) The country had only 598 physicians in 2002.[[224]](#cite_note-227)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Culture")]

Namibian culture is similar to South African culture due to their tied history and family nationalities. Few Namibians express interest in permanently settling in other countries; they prefer the safety of their homeland, have a strong national identity, and enjoy a well-supplied retail sector.[[225]](#cite_note-228) Namibians are typically very social and are consistently among the highest alcohol consumption rates per capita, and ranked first in Africa for beer consumption per capita.[[226]](#cite_note-229)

### Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Sport")]

Main articles: [Sport in Namibia](/wiki/Sport_in_Namibia "Sport in Namibia") and [Rugby union in Namibia](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Namibia "Rugby union in Namibia")

The most popular sport in Namibia is [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"). The [Namibia national football team](/wiki/Namibia_national_football_team "Namibia national football team") qualified for the [1998](/wiki/1998_Africa_Cup_of_Nations "1998 Africa Cup of Nations"), [2008](/wiki/2008_Africa_Cup_of_Nations "2008 Africa Cup of Nations"), [2019](/wiki/2019_Africa_Cup_of_Nations "2019 Africa Cup of Nations"), and [2023](/wiki/2023_Africa_Cup_of_Nations "2023 Africa Cup of Nations") editions of the [Africa Cup of Nations](/wiki/Africa_Cup_of_Nations "Africa Cup of Nations"), but has yet to qualify for the [World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup"). Some notable players include [Derby County](/wiki/Derby_County_F.C. "Derby County F.C.") right-back [Ryan Nyambe](/wiki/Ryan_Nyambe "Ryan Nyambe"), [Mamelodi Sundowns](/wiki/Mamelodi_Sundowns_F.C. "Mamelodi Sundowns F.C.") forward [Peter Shalulile](/wiki/Peter_Shalulile "Peter Shalulile"), and retired footballer [Collin Benjamin](/wiki/Collin_Benjamin "Collin Benjamin").

The most successful national team is the [Namibian rugby team](/wiki/Namibia_national_rugby_union_team "Namibia national rugby union team"), having competed in the last seven World Cups. Namibia were participants in the [1999](/wiki/1999_Rugby_World_Cup "1999 Rugby World Cup"), [2003](/wiki/2003_Rugby_World_Cup "2003 Rugby World Cup"), [2007](/wiki/2007_Rugby_World_Cup "2007 Rugby World Cup"), [2011](/wiki/2011_Rugby_World_Cup "2011 Rugby World Cup"), [2015](/wiki/2015_Rugby_World_Cup "2015 Rugby World Cup"), [2019](/wiki/2019_Rugby_World_Cup "2019 Rugby World Cup"), and most recent [2023](/wiki/2023_Rugby_World_Cup "2023 Rugby World Cup") [Rugby World Cup](/wiki/Rugby_World_Cup "Rugby World Cup"). [Jacques Burger](/wiki/Jacques_Burger "Jacques Burger") is an internationally successful Namibian rugby player.

[Cricket in Namibia](/wiki/Cricket_in_Namibia "Cricket in Namibia") is also popular, with [the national side](/wiki/Namibia_national_cricket_team "Namibia national cricket team") having qualified for [2003 Cricket World Cup](/wiki/2003_Cricket_World_Cup "2003 Cricket World Cup"), [2021 ICC T20 World Cup](/wiki/2021_ICC_T20_World_Cup "2021 ICC T20 World Cup") and [2022 ICC Men's T20 World Cup](/wiki/2022_ICC_Men%27s_T20_World_Cup "2022 ICC Men's T20 World Cup").[[227]](#cite_note-230) In December 2017, Namibia Cricket reached the final of the Cricket South Africa (CSA) Provincial One Day Challenge for the first time.[[228]](#cite_note-NCYR-231) In February 2018, Namibia hosted the ICC World Cricket League Division 2 with Namibia, Kenya, UAE, Nepal, Canada and Oman to compete for the final two ICC Cricket World Cup Qualifier positions in Zimbabwe. Namibia also qualified the qualifiers of ICC T20 World Cup 2021 and entered the super 12 club.[[228]](#cite_note-NCYR-231)

Other Namibians have achieved notable success in individual sports. [Frankie Fredericks](/wiki/Frankie_Fredericks "Frankie Fredericks"), sprinter in the 100 and 200 m [track and field](/wiki/Track_and_field "Track and field") events, has won four Olympic silver medals (1992, 1996) and has medals from several [World Athletics Championships](/wiki/IAAF_World_Championships_in_Athletics "IAAF World Championships in Athletics").[[229]](#cite_note-232) Golfer [Trevor Dodds](/wiki/Trevor_Dodds "Trevor Dodds") won the [Greater Greensboro Open](/wiki/Greater_Greensboro_Open "Greater Greensboro Open") in 1998, one of 15 tournaments in his career. He achieved a career-high world ranking of 78th in 1998.[[230]](#cite_note-233) Professional cyclist and Namibian Road Race champion [Dan Craven](/wiki/Dan_Craven "Dan Craven") represented Namibia at the [2016 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2016_Summer_Olympics "2016 Summer Olympics") in both the road race and individual time trial.[[231]](#cite_note-234) Boxer [Julius Indongo](/wiki/Julius_Indongo "Julius Indongo") was a unified WBA, IBF, and IBO world champion in the [Light welterweight](/wiki/Light_welterweight "Light welterweight") division.

### Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Media")]

See also: [Media of Namibia](/wiki/Media_of_Namibia "Media of Namibia")

Compared to neighbouring countries, Namibia has a large degree of media freedom. Over the past years, the country usually ranked in the upper quarter of the [Press Freedom Index](/wiki/Press_Freedom_Index "Press Freedom Index") of [Reporters without Borders](/wiki/Reporters_without_Borders "Reporters without Borders"), reaching position 21 in 2010, being on par with Canada and the best-positioned African country.[[232]](#cite_note-235) The African Media Barometer shows similarly positive results. However, as in other countries, there is still mentionable influence of representatives of state and economy on media in Namibia.[[233]](#cite_note-Rothe-236) In 2009, Namibia dropped to position 36 on the Press Freedom Index.[[234]](#cite_note-237) In 2013, it was 19th,[[235]](#cite_note-238) 22nd in 2014[[236]](#cite_note-239) and 23rd in 2019,[[237]](#cite_note-240) meaning that it is currently the highest ranked African country in terms of press freedom.

Although Namibia's population is fairly small, the country has a diverse choice of media; two TV stations, 19 radio stations (without counting community stations), 5 daily newspapers, several weeklies and special publications compete for the attention of the audience. Additionally, a mentionable amount of foreign media, especially South African, is available. Online media are mostly based on print publication content. Namibia has a state-owned press agency, called [NAMPA](/wiki/Namibia_Press_Agency "Namibia Press Agency").[[233]](#cite_note-Rothe-236) Overall c. 300 journalists work in the country.[[238]](#cite_note-jobs-241)

The first newspaper in Namibia was the German-language *Windhoeker Anzeiger*, founded 1898. During German rule, the newspapers mainly reflected the living reality and the view of the white German-speaking minority. The black majority was ignored or depicted as a threat. During South African rule, the white bias continued, with mentionable influence of the Pretoria government on the South West African media system. Independent newspapers were seen as a menace to the existing order, and critical journalists were often threatened.[[233]](#cite_note-Rothe-236)[[239]](#cite_note-242)[[240]](#cite_note-Links-243)

Current daily newspapers are the private publications *[The Namibian](/wiki/The_Namibian "The Namibian")* (English and other languages), *[Die Republikein](/wiki/Die_Republikein "Die Republikein")* (Afrikaans), *[Allgemeine Zeitung](/wiki/Allgemeine_Zeitung_(Namibia) "Allgemeine Zeitung (Namibia)")* (German) and *[Namibian Sun](/wiki/Namibian_Sun "Namibian Sun")* (English) as well as the state-owned *[New Era](/wiki/New_Era_(Namibia) "New Era (Namibia)")* (predominantly English). Except for the largest newspaper, *The Namibian*, which is owned by a trust, the other mentioned private newspapers are part of Democratic Media Holdings.[[233]](#cite_note-Rothe-236) Other mentionable newspapers are the tabloid *Informanté* owned by TrustCo, the weekly *[Windhoek Observer](/wiki/Windhoek_Observer "Windhoek Observer")*, the weekly *[Namibia Economist](/wiki/Namibia_Economist "Namibia Economist")*, as well as the regional *Namib Times*. Current affairs magazines include *[Insight Namibia](/wiki/Insight_Namibia "Insight Namibia")*, *Vision2030 Focus magazine*[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and *Prime FOCUS*. The *[Sister Namibia](/wiki/Sister_Namibia "Sister Namibia")* magazine stands out as the longest running NGO magazine in Namibia, while *[Namibia Sport](/wiki/Namibia_Sport "Namibia Sport")* is the only national sport magazine. Furthermore, the print market is complemented with party publications, student newspapers and PR publications.[[233]](#cite_note-Rothe-236)

Radio was introduced in 1969, TV in 1981. The broadcasting sector today is dominated by the state-run [Namibian Broadcasting Corporation](/wiki/Namibian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Namibian Broadcasting Corporation") (NBC). The [public broadcaster](/wiki/Public_broadcasting "Public broadcasting") offers a TV station as well as a "National Radio" in English and nine language services in locally spoken languages. The nine private radio stations in the country are mainly English-language channels, except for Radio Omulunga (Oshiwambo) and Kosmos 94.1 (Afrikaans).
Privately held [One Africa TV](/wiki/One_Africa_Television "One Africa Television") has competed with NBC since the 2000s.[[233]](#cite_note-Rothe-236)[[241]](#cite_note-oneafricabackground-244)

Media and journalists in Namibia are represented by the Namibian chapter of the [Media Institute of Southern Africa](/wiki/Media_Institute_of_Southern_Africa "Media Institute of Southern Africa") and the Editors' Forum of Namibia. An independent media ombudsman was appointed in 2009 to prevent a state-controlled media council.[[233]](#cite_note-Rothe-236)

### Art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Namibia&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Art")]

The [National Art Gallery of Namibia](/wiki/National_Art_Gallery_of_Namibia "National Art Gallery of Namibia") houses a permanent exhibition of Namibian, African and European Art and shows temporary exhibitions of local artists. In 2022 Namibia took part in the [Venice Biennale](/wiki/Venice_Biennale "Venice Biennale") for the first time. It competed in the [59th Edition](/wiki/59th_Venice_Biennale "59th Venice Biennale") with the exhibition "A Bridge to the Desert" featuring the "[Lone Stone Men](/w/index.php?title=Lone_Stone_Men&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lone Stone Men (page does not exist)")" project by the anonymous [RENN](/w/index.php?title=RENN_(Namibian_artist)&action=edit&redlink=1 "RENN (Namibian artist) (page does not exist)").[[242]](#cite_note-245)

A substantial minority of Herero crossed the Kalahari desert into the British colony of Bechuanaland (modern-day Botswana), where a small community continues to live in western Botswana near to the border with Namibia. *Let us die fighting*, originally published (1966) under the title *Südwestafrika unter deutscher Kolonialherrschaft*.
